findings.description: Table of contents table structure is not preserved.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, page 2 shows a simple two-column table of contents with section titles aligned to page numbers. In the HTML, the same content is rendered as a grid-style table with visible cell borders and row segmentation, and the structure is split across multiple screenshots/pages, altering the visual table layout substantially.

findings.description: Cover page layout is not preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF cover page centers the title block vertically with large whitespace and logo at the bottom. The HTML places all content top-left in a linear flow, losing the original centered cover-page composition and whitespace grouping.

findings.description: Cover page text styling and alignment differ from the original.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses centered title text with varying font sizes and emphasis; the HTML renders the same text left-aligned with flatter typography, reducing the distinction of the cover hierarchy.

findings.description: Table of contents page layout is altered from document-style layout to web-style layout.
findings.evidence: The PDF table of contents is centered on the page with wide margins and sparse spacing. The HTML expands content to full width with a large web heading and boxed table, changing margins and whitespace grouping.

findings.description: Table of contents formatting differs from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses underlined section labels and a simple typeset page-number alignment. The HTML uses bold header cells and browser-style table formatting, changing the visual distinction of the contents.

findings.description: Page layout is simplified from document page format to continuous web text.
findings.evidence: On the introduction pages, the PDF uses narrower text blocks with document margins and explicit section placement. The HTML uses full-width paragraphs in a continuous layout, changing whitespace and page composition but preserving content order.

findings.description: Section heading and body text styling differ.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif fonts and a more formal section-title presentation; the HTML uses sans-serif web fonts with larger bold headings, which changes but does not obscure structure.

findings.description: Continuation page layout differs from the source PDF.
findings.evidence: Page 4 in PDF continues body text in a fixed-width document column, while the HTML uses a wider continuous text block with different margins and spacing.

findings.description: Section 2 page layout is simplified in HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF places the section heading and subsection with traditional document margins; the HTML presents them in a wider web layout. Content remains in the same order.

findings.description: Typography differs for headings and body text.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif type and tighter heading styling; the HTML uses large sans-serif headings and more open line spacing.

findings.description: Schematic map image is resized and partially repositioned in HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 6, the PDF shows the full map centered on the page. The HTML places the image within the text flow and at a different scale, though the graphic remains present and legible.

findings.description: Image page layout differs from source.
findings.evidence: The PDF dedicates most of page 6 to the schematic image with generous margins. The HTML integrates the image into a continuous scrolling layout, reducing whitespace and changing placement relative to the page.

findings.description: Section layout on model input page is rendered as continuous web content.
findings.evidence: The PDF separates subsection heading, underlined subheads, and paragraphs in a document page layout. The HTML preserves order but uses web spacing and full-width text.

findings.description: Underlined subsection formatting is not faithfully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF clearly underlines subheads such as 'Static and Pattern Data'; the HTML shows heading-style text without matching underline emphasis in the same way.

findings.description: Page structure for model input continuation differs from source document layout.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses distinct underlined subsection headers and narrow page text; the HTML continues in a broader web layout with looser spacing.

findings.description: Subsection heading emphasis differs from the original.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents 'Timeseries Data' and 'Operating Rules' as underlined subsection labels; the HTML uses heading styling without fully matching the original typographic treatment.

findings.description: Continuation page rendered as plain web paragraph flow.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 9 retains document margins and centered page composition, while the HTML shows the same text in a continuous full-width column.

findings.description: Flow chart image is preserved but rescaled and repositioned.
findings.evidence: On page 10, the PDF centers the 'Upper Cape Fear Basin' flow chart as a standalone page. The HTML includes the diagram below preceding text at a different scale within web flow.

findings.description: Standalone diagram page converted to embedded web section.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a full page for the diagram; the HTML embeds it into continuous reading flow, changing whitespace grouping and page-level structure.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but resized in HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 11, the middle Cape Fear flow chart appears centered and full-page in the PDF, while the HTML shows it as an embedded image with different scaling.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but repositioned and scaled differently.
findings.evidence: On page 12, the Deep River flow chart is preserved in HTML but appears embedded in the text flow rather than as a standalone centered page.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but layout differs from original.
findings.evidence: On page 13, the Lower Cape Fear flow chart remains visible, but in HTML it is placed within the web reading stream rather than as a dedicated page with wide margins.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but scaled and positioned differently.
findings.evidence: On page 14, the Upper Neuse flow chart is visible in HTML but rendered as an embedded web image rather than a standalone page composition.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but page-level layout is not preserved.
findings.evidence: On page 15, the Middle Neuse flow chart appears in HTML, though embedded into a continuous layout with reduced page whitespace compared with the PDF.

findings.description: Flow chart image is present but not in the same standalone layout.
findings.evidence: On page 16, the Lower Neuse flow chart is included and legible, but the HTML places it inside the page flow instead of preserving the original centered standalone page layout.

findings.description: Run configurations page layout is simplified.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses document margins and underlined subsection headings on page 17; the HTML presents a wider text block and web-styled headings while keeping order intact.

findings.description: Subsection formatting is not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows subsection labels like 'Simulation:' and 'Position Analysis:' with distinctive underlined styling; the HTML uses bold/web heading treatment instead.

findings.description: Model output page uses continuous web layout instead of document page composition.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 18 has a document-like text block with clear margins and subsection placement. The HTML renders the same content in a wider web column with different spacing.

findings.description: Text styling and emphasis differ from the PDF on the final page.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif typography and conventional document emphasis for section and body text. The HTML uses sans-serif web styling and different spacing, though the content remains readable.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy does not reflect the document structure. The HTML uses multiple h1 elements for major sections and omits the document title as the top-level h1, which breaks the structural outline.
findings.evidence:
PDF title page shows the document title 'Modeling the Cape Fear and Neuse River Basin Operations with OASIS' as the primary title, with later section headings such as 'Table of Contents', 'Section 1. Introduction', and 'Section 2. Model Components'. HTML instead uses plain

for the document title and multiple

tags for 'Table of Contents', 'Section 1. Introduction', and 'Section 2. Model Components'.

findings.description: A sentence in Section 2.1 is textually corrupted, changing the meaning of the node count description.
findings.evidence: PDF page 5 reads: 'There are 58 reservoir nodes (42 actual reservoirs, 11 flood impoundments on Crabtree Creek, and five artificial storage nodes used for time-of-travel flow routing)...' HTML reads: 'There are 58 reservoir 42 actual reservoirs, 11 flood impoundments on Crabtree Creek, and five artificial storage nodes used for time-of-travel flow routing)...' The word 'nodes (' is missing, creating a malformed and inaccurate sentence.
